title: Ironsworn: Starforged - Part 1 - inspiration and session 0 (solo RPG)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PLFNoideC9s

Daniel (@dungeondive) kicks off a short Sci-Fi Month series with the first part of his Starforged campaign: the reading pile that fuelled it, and the session-zero work that built the world before the dice ever rolled. He pulls books from his shelves and explains why each one earned the slot — Callahan's Cross-Time Saloon (for the toast as iron vow), The Stainless Steel Rat, Harrison's Deathworld Trilogy, Bradbury's Martian Chronicles, the Strugatskys' Roadside Picnic, Haldeman's Mindbridge, and Simak's City (the seed for a non-human companion). He then disagrees with Starforged's reputation as a beginner solo game — it's structured, but it's deep.

The session-zero work is where this episode really lives. Daniel walks through the Truths worksheets and builds a setting: interdimensional "divine" entities that raptured his ancestors then turned out to be physical lifeforms seeding mythologies to enslave worlds. His character, Quint Savoy, is a myth theorist — the secular counterpart to a theologian — sworn upon a Black Iron icon called the Icon of Truth. Ship: the Jackdaw. Companion: a Banshee. Opening vow: investigate a religious relic on the jungle world Damu, suspected source of a plague creeping toward Rock Home Port. Roll yields a weak hit; the quest begins with more questions than answers.

----

Starforged's session-zero Truths worksheets do a lot of heavy lifting before play even starts — does that kind of structured world-prep help you commit to a solo campaign, or does it feel like homework before the game?
